header.tagline: Reading the Pacific through its data

home.subtitle: A dive into Pacific data: a sea that rises, that warms, and the peoples who carry its weight.
home.live_loading: Connecting to the Pacific Data Hub…
home.acts_intro: Eleven acts, a region in action.

acts.a1_text: The Pacific emits a tiny fraction of global gases. Understanding that gap lays the first stone of a fair response.
acts.a2_text: Sea-surface temperature anomaly, by territory and year (°C, departure from the reference). A single physical signal, precisely measured.
acts.a3_text: Sea level rises by EEZ (anomaly, m) while population grows or declines (%). We cross the two: where does exposure concentrate?
acts.a4_text: Behind the numbers, people — and a regional solidarity taking shape in the face of disasters.
acts.a5_text: The Pacific does not merely endure: the rise of clean energy traces a path forward.
acts.a6_text: Crops and livestock: how the islands adapt their farming to keep food security.
acts.a7_text: Red List and fisheries management: biodiversity under pressure, and the concrete efforts to preserve it.
acts.a8_text: Rainfall, temperatures, monitoring network: observing the atmosphere better to prepare for it better.
acts.a9_text: Tourism, electricity, green taxation: the economic levers of a sustainable transition.
acts.a10_text: Access to drinking water and health: tangible progress closest to people.
acts.a11_text: Everything crossed: responsibility and vulnerability, to light where the Pacific is advancing and where to focus effort.

home.thesis: The Pacific's climate story in eleven acts: not a fate, but a region that measures, adapts and acts — told through its own data.
home.shock_text: of global emissions, yet one of the loudest voices for climate. The Pacific turns its vulnerability into leadership.
home.acts_lead: From emissions to solutions, each act crosses the Pacific's open data to show a reality — and the response rising to meet it. Follow the thread: it leads to action.
home.closing: “Where others see a threat, the Pacific is already charting solutions.”
home.c1_desc: Reading what the raw numbers say: what the Pacific emits, what the ocean sends back, the human challenge taking shape. Clear facts, the basis of every solution.
home.c2_desc: Understanding the response behind the data: energy, agriculture, biodiversity, atmosphere, economy. Five fronts where the Pacific innovates and adapts.
home.c3_desc: Reading the future closest to people — water, health — then the synthesis that lights the way: what's advancing, and where to focus effort.
home.stat4_text: to read a region that adapts
home.m1_desc: Greenhouse-gas emissions per capita, territory by territory, set against the world average and the major emitters.
home.m2_desc: The measured signals of disruption: sea-surface temperature, rainfall and land-temperature anomalies, and the observation network.
home.m3_desc: What the climate does to resources: crop yields, the species-extinction index, and fisheries management.
home.m4_desc: Where the figures meet the inhabitants: sea level on populated coasts, drinking-water access, disease, and the toll of disasters.
home.m5_desc: The levers of adaptation — renewable share, economic transformation — and the synthesis that crosses responsibility with vulnerability.

intro.lead: The Pacific is on the front line of warming. Datamoana tells that story — through data.
intro.body1: Built as a website in several acts, the platform draws on as much open data as possible to shed light on every facet of the regional climate challenge: rising seas, a warming ocean, food autonomy, health, the economy.
intro.body2: The aim is not only to document, but to understand the mechanisms and open paths to solutions for the peoples of the Pacific — the most exposed, and already on the move.
intro.p1_text: Open, real, verifiable data across the 22 territories.
intro.p2_text: Eleven acts connecting the figures to realities lived on the ground.
intro.p3_text: Giving decision-makers and communities what they need to build solutions.

modes.lead: The idea: stay accessible and understandable to as many people as possible. Pick your way in.
modes.browse_text: Explore freely: pick the act that speaks to you and dive straight in.
modes.guided_text: “Start the experience” takes you through the eleven acts, in narrative order.
modes.fun_text: “Did you know?”: striking facts drawn from real data, to enter through curiosity.

keyfigures.source: Source: Pacific Data Hub · Pacific Community (SPC)

teaser.title: 22 territories, one front line
teaser.lead: From the largest to the most remote, every Pacific territory faces climate change. Step into the story, act by act.

method.title: Real, traceable figures
method.lead: This platform relies only on open, verifiable data. No value is made up: when data is missing, we say so.
method.sources_text: Data from the Pacific Data Hub and the Pacific Community (SPC), across the 22 territories.
method.integrity_text: Records and trends are computed live from official series. No dressed-up numbers, no hidden extrapolation.
method.tech_text: React, interactive mapping, ECharts & ApexCharts visualizations, GPU animations (canvas, GSAP).

closing_cta.title: Step into the Pacific’s story
closing_cta.text: Eleven acts, real data, a region on the front line. It’s up to you how to read it.

footer.tagline: Giving a face to climate-change data across the Pacific.
footer.scope: Scope: all Pacific Island countries and territories (SPC).
footer.credit: Built for the Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026.
footer.challenge_aria: Open the Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026 website

act1.lead: How much does each Pacific territory emit per person? A clear read — territory by territory, year after year — of the region's emissions: the groundwork for action.
act1.chart_sub: by territory · hover a dot for details
act1.caption: Source: Pacific Data Hub (.Stat) — SPC. Per-capita values.
act1.paradox: Most Pacific territories emit little per capita, and their share of global emissions is tiny. Yet they are among the first exposed to rising seas and warming.
act1.empty: No usable data for this dataset.
act1.map_no_token: Map unavailable: add your Mapbox token to a .env file (REACT_APP_MAPBOX_TOKEN=…) at the project root, then restart “npm start”.
act1.viz_rank_sub: bars reorder by year — who rises, who falls
act1.viz_trend_sub: evolution of the biggest emitters · the cursor tracks the year

guide.title: How to read this
guide.intro: This act compares per-capita GHG emissions across Pacific territories, set against the climate impact they endure.

steps.v: The subregion pills (Melanesia / Polynesia / Micronesia) refocus every per-territory view. "All" keeps the 17.
Each bar is a territory (SPC code). Longer = more per capita. The scale is compressed to stay readable despite extremes (Palau, New Caledonia).
The line marks the Pacific MEDIAN — more robust than a mean skewed by a few outliers.
The scatter crosses what a territory emits (x) with what it endures (y): most emit little yet are highly exposed.

guide.takeaway: The paradox: emitting little, yet first to face climate change.

evo.title: Evolution over time
evo.sub: change between the first and last available years
evo.no_data: Not enough history to analyse the evolution.

act1.outlier_note: sit off the scale: tiny population denominators, diesel electricity and industry. Yet their combined share of global emissions stays negligible.

subregion.title: Average by subregion
subregion.sub: average trajectory of the three Pacific subregions

change.title: Biggest movers since
change.sub: change in per-capita emissions between first and last available year

scatter.title: Emit little, suffer most
scatter.sub: each bubble = a territory · left = low emitter · higher = more exposed
scatter.empty: Per-territory impact data unavailable — run "node scripts/fetch-pdh.mjs" or retry.

heatmap.title: The big picture
heatmap.sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = emission intensity

viz.trend_sub: Per-capita emissions over time, by territory
viz.heat_sub: Per-capita emission intensity across the whole period
viz.sub_sub: Average per-capita emissions — Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia
viz.change_sub: Change between the first and last available years
viz.table_sub: Values by territory for the selected year
viz.box_sub: Spread of per-capita emissions across territories, each year (box = central 50%)
viz.river_sub: Sub-region average over time, as a stacked flow
viz.tree_sub: Per-capita emissions for the selected year, grouped by sub-region
viz.race_sub: Animated ranking of per-capita emissions, year after year
viz.bump_sub: Each territory's position in the ranking, over time
viz.scatter_sub: Current emissions (horizontal) and change since the first year (vertical)

story.rank: The vast majority of territories sit below the regional median — among the lightest footprints on the planet.
story.trend: Broadly contained trajectories: the region keeps emissions low even as it develops.
story.map: From one archipelago to the next, the per-capita footprint stays modest — an asset for the regional transition.
story.heat: Seen in one glance, the period confirms a durably low emission intensity.
story.sub: The three sub-regions share a sober profile, with gaps that open the door to mutual learning.
story.change: For many territories, per-capita emissions hold steady or fall — an encouraging signal.
story.box: Most territories cluster in a low band — a handful of special cases alone pull the average upward.
story.river: Seen as a flow, the regional profile stays steady and sober from year to year.
story.tree: The hierarchy shows at a glance where emissions concentrate — and how many territories stay very sober.
story.race: Fast-forwarded across half a century, you see who moves and who stays sober — most territories keep a modest footprint.
story.bump: Beyond the values, ranking positions barely move: regional profiles are remarkably stable.
story.radar: From one decade to the next, the three sub-regions keep a sober, tightly-grouped profile.
story.scatter: Bottom-left: low emissions trending down — that's where most territories gather.

f.scale_help: The scale affects the line charts (Trend): in log, each gridline is ×10, making small values readable. The ranking stays linear, since bars must start from zero.
f.color_help: By rank: colour follows the ranking (every territory stands out). Absolute: colour follows the raw value (an extreme can flatten everything).

outro.title: The groundwork is laid
outro.text: The Pacific emits little per capita — bar a few exceptions. Now to what those emissions trigger: the ocean, on the front line.

act1.thesis: Across the Pacific, per-capita emissions vary tenfold — and rarely where you'd think.

board.rank_find: Per capita, the gap between territories runs tenfold — and the order surprises.
board.rank_take: The biggest per-head emitters aren't always the ones you'd expect.
board.race_find: The ranking replayed year by year: who climbs, who falls back.
board.race_take: In a few decades, the emitters' hierarchy has been reshuffled.
board.trend_find: Individual trajectories: some rise, others level off or fall.
board.trend_take: No single path: the Pacific moves in scattered order.
board.scatter_find: Current level (X) against change since the start (Y), by sub-region.
board.scatter_take: Emitting little today doesn't prevent rising fast — and vice versa.
board.map_find: The geography of per-capita emissions, year by year.
board.map_take: Per-capita peaks often sit in small territories.
board.heat_find: Each row a territory, each column a year: intensity over time.
board.heat_take: The hottest bands reveal episodes of sustained emissions.
board.change_find: Net change in per-capita emissions between the first and the last year of data.
board.change_take: In green, those who cut their per-head emissions; in red, those who raised them.

act2.lead: Two silent signals are redrawing the Pacific's horizon: rising sea level and warming surface temperature. Here is the retrieved data, territory by territory.
act2.coming: Data retrieved ✓ — visualisations in preparation. The full page comes next.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: Two indicators, one story: the Pacific ocean is rising and warming. Both curves show ANOMALIES — the gap from a reference period, not an absolute value.

steps.v: 0 = the reference normal. Above 0: warmer / higher than usual; below: cooler / lower.
The thick line is the average of the 21 territories; the light band shows the gap between the lowest and highest territory that year.
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on both curves AND the map at once.
Blue = below reference, red = above; circle size reflects magnitude. Pick the metric with the toggle.

guide.takeaway: Slow but unmistakable, both curves cross the reference and never come back: the Pacific is warming and the sea is rising.

act2.evo_sub: change between first and last available years
act2.heatmap_sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = deviation from normal
act2.change_sub: difference between the first and last available years
act2.caption: Sources: Pacific Data Hub / SPC — sea-level anomaly (ref. 1993–2012) and sea-surface temperature anomaly.

viz.band_sub: Regional mean and spread across territories, year by year
viz.rank_sub: Anomaly per territory for the selected year
viz.map_sub: Anomaly per territory — blue below reference, red above
viz.heat_sub: Every territory, every year, by intensity
viz.box_sub: Distribution of anomalies across territories, per year
viz.river_sub: Mean anomaly per sub-region over time
viz.change_sub: Gap between the first and last observed year
viz.dual_sub: Sea level (left axis) and surface temperature (right axis)
viz.share_sub: Share of territories above reference, year by year
viz.dumb_sub: First observed year → last, per territory

story.band: The line follows the territories' mean; the band shows the gap between lowest and highest. Above 0, the ocean exceeds its reference.
story.rank: Most territories now sit above reference — exposure is uneven.
story.map: Across the Pacific the anomaly reads at a glance: almost everywhere above normal.
story.heat: Bands warm from left to right: the trend is clear over time.
story.corr: Territories rising most are often those warming most — two signals of one disruption.
story.box: Year after year the distribution shifts upward: median and extremes rise.
story.river: The three sub-regions follow the same upward slope, at similar rates.
story.change: Between the first and last year, nearly every territory shows an increase.
story.dual: Both lines rise together: the Pacific warms as it rises. Two measures, one disruption.
story.share: The share of territories above normal climbs over time — the anomaly no longer touches only a few islands.
story.dumb: The muted dot marks the first year, the warm dot the last: the gap is the distance travelled.

outro.title: The ocean has spoken
outro.text: A warmer sea feeds a more unstable atmosphere. On to the sky: rainfall and temperature anomalies over land.

act2.thesis: Sea-surface temperature anomaly (°C, departure from the reference), territory by territory: the trend is positive across nearly every Pacific EEZ.

board.band_find: Mean SST anomaly and the spread across territories, year by year.
board.band_take: The curve crosses the reference and stays above: a clear, lasting warming.
board.rank_find: Departure from the reference, territory by territory, at the chosen year.
board.rank_take: Almost every territory sits above the reference — exposure is widespread.
board.map_find: The geography of the anomaly across the archipelago, year by year.
board.map_take: No territory is spared; the lowest atolls are the most vulnerable.
board.corr_find: The Pacific's path year by year: warming (X) and sea level (Y) climb together.
board.corr_take: From cool-and-low toward warm-and-high: one single ascent, two linked symptoms.
board.heat_find: Each row a territory, each column a year: intensity over time.
board.heat_take: Bands darken toward the present — the anomaly settles in everywhere.
board.dual_find: Both measures on one chart, each with its own axis.
board.dual_take: Sea level and temperature climb together, decade after decade.
board.change_find: Net change between the first and the last year of data.
board.change_take: In green the anomaly easing; in red the one getting worse.

act3.lead: As the sea advances, the human map is redrawn. Population growth is slowing almost everywhere — and several islands are already losing inhabitants, while others crowd a growing population onto a threatened shore.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: The indicator is an annual population GROWTH RATE (in %), not a headcount. 0 = stable population; above 0: the island is gaining inhabitants; below: it is losing them.

steps.v: Each value is the one-year population change, in %. Negative = decline (emigration), positive = growth.
The thick line is the average of the 22 territories; the band shows where 8 in 10 territories sit (10th–90th percentile), so it isn't crushed by tiny islands with extreme swings.
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on the curve AND the 3D map at once.
Height ∝ rate magnitude; red = the island is growing (coastal pressure), blue = it is emptying (departures).

guide.takeaway: Growth is running out of steam: many islands plateau or empty out, while a handful pack ever more people onto increasingly exposed coasts.

act3.evo_sub: change in rate between first and last year
act3.diverge_sub: current-year rate — green: gaining people · red: losing them
act3.dumb_sub: first → last observed year, per territory
act3.heat_sub: annual rate — green: growth · red: decline

story.trend: The regional mean runs out of breath; the band shows the gap between territories that grow and those that empty out.
story.map: On the map, relief tells the scale: red, the island densifies on an exposed coast; blue, it loses people.
story.diverge: In the chosen year, some territories still gain inhabitants while others already tip into decline.
story.dumb: From one end of the period to the other, read the distance travelled: most trajectories slide downward.
story.heat: Year after year, green gives way to red: the slowdown reads at a glance.
story.evo: Who accelerates, who falls behind: the change in rate between the first and last year.
story.table: Every value, sortable, to explore territory by territory.
story.gauge: Only a minority of islands still gain inhabitants; slide through the years to watch the share melt away.
story.balance: Year after year the columns dip below the line: declining territories gradually outnumber growing ones.

outro.title: Coasts under pressure
outro.text: Ever more people on rising coasts. Closest to them now: the water they drink and the disease that threatens them.

act3.gauge_sub: share of territories growing, in the current year
act3.balance_sub: number of territories growing vs declining, per year
act3.thesis: Sea level by EEZ (anomaly in m, ref. 1993–2012) crossed with the population growth rate (%): exposure concentrates where the sea rises and people stay.

board.trend_find: The band shows the regional spread (10–90%) around the mean, year by year.
board.trend_take: Overall momentum is easing, but gaps between territories stay wide.
board.lines_find: Each line a territory: read who accelerates, who slows, who declines.
board.lines_take: Trajectories diverge — sustained growth here, depopulation there.
board.rank_find: Sea-level anomaly, territory by territory, for the selected year.
board.rank_take: Almost all territories sit above the reference: exposure is widespread.
board.map_find: The geography of the sea-level anomaly for the selected year.
board.map_take: Almost every EEZ sits above the reference; low atolls are the most vulnerable.
board.evo_find: Who accelerated most and who slowed most over the whole period.
board.evo_take: Reversals reveal the territories under pressure.
board.cross_find: Each dot is a territory: latest sea anomaly (X) against the population growth rate (Y).
board.cross_take: Top-right: the sea rises and population still grows — that is where exposure builds up.
board.band_find: Mean sea-level anomaly and the spread across territories, year by year.
board.band_take: The curve crosses the reference and stays above: the sea settles above its normal.
board.pop_find: Latest population growth rate, territory by territory.
board.pop_take: Several islands still gain people while others empty out: two opposing pressures on the coast.
board.profile_find: Relative rank (0–100) for each territory: sea level (left) against the population growth rate (right).
board.profile_take: Long bars on both sides flag the most exposed: high sea AND a still-growing population.
board.path_find: For each territory, the growth rate from the first observed year (dull dot) to the most recent (warm dot).
board.path_take: Most paths drift downward: growth slows but stays positive on rising coasts.
board.coast_find: Each dot is a satellite-tracked coastline segment (Landsat, 1999–2023): red is retreat, blue is growth, expressed in metres per year.
board.coast_take: Retreat is not uniform along the coast: it concentrates on specific stretches, often among the most inhabited.
board.coastbal_find: For each territory, the share of coastline retreating (red, left) versus growing (blue, right), aggregated from satellite-tracked segments.
board.coastbal_take: French Polynesia, American Samoa and Papua New Guinea lean toward retreat; conversely, several atolls (Tuvalu, Federated States of Micronesia) are gaining ground — erosion is not a uniform fate.
board.coastmap_find: Per-territory map: column height and colour intensity show the share of coastline in retreat.
board.coastmap_take: Retreat hotspots concentrate on a few territories; elsewhere the coast stays stable or advances.
board.slope_find: For each territory, sea level at the first and last observed year (mm anomaly). A rising slope = rising sea.
board.slope_take: The rise is near-universal and of similar magnitude across territories: the sea is rising for (almost) everyone.
board.spread_find: Each box summarises a territory's distribution of coastline-change rates (median, quartiles, extremes), in m/year.
board.spread_take: A single coast often mixes retreat and growth: the spread reveals the heterogeneity that averages hide.
board.bubble_find: Cross-view: x = population growth, y = share of coastline retreating, bubble size = number of tracked segments.
board.bubble_take: Where population grows fast and the coast retreats, pressures compound — those are the territories to watch first.

viz.cross_title: Exposure: the sea rises, people stay
viz.band_title: The sea rises, year by year
viz.coastbal_title: Retreat or growth: the balance by territory

coast.attr: Coastline: Digital Earth Pacific — Landsat Coastlines (CC BY-NC 4.0)

act4.timeline_sub: one circle per event, sized by magnitude

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: Here the data isn't a steady trend but a series of DISASTERS: isolated spikes, many years with no event. So we look at totals and shocks, not a curve.

steps.v: Each disaster is a dated event: people affected and economic losses cluster in a few years.
Each circle is an event placed on its year; the bigger it is, the more people it affected (or the more it cost). Clusters reveal the dark years.
The total over the whole period shows which territories carry the heaviest toll.
Heights are on a logarithmic scale: without it, Fiji would dwarf everyone else. Taller and redder means a higher total.

guide.takeaway: A handful of events — Winston (Fiji, 2016), Pam (Vanuatu, 2015) — concentrate most of the human cost: hundreds of thousands of people affected and hundreds of millions of dollars in losses.

act4.lead: Anomalies always end up with a face: people affected and economic losses, concentrated in a few disaster years.
act4.dual_sub: people affected (left) and losses (right), per year

story.timeline: Each circle is a disaster: few events, but some enormous. The rhythm is nothing like a smooth curve.
story.annual: Aggregated by year, the spikes stand out: a few years concentrate most of the damage.
story.dual: Human cost and economic cost often rise together — two faces of one shock.
story.rank: Over the whole period, a few territories pile up a far heavier toll than the rest.
story.pareto: The cumulative line climbs fast: a handful of territories makes up most of the total.
story.treemap: Area shows the share: at a glance, you see who takes the most.
story.heat: The grid reveals the one-off shocks — when and where they struck, beyond the totals.
story.map: On the map, the cumulative footprint reads in relief: a logarithmic scale so small territories aren't crushed.

outro.title: The human cost is real
outro.text: Behind the anomalies, lives and whole economies are upended. Next: whether the region is taking back control.

act4.thesis: Emitting almost nothing hasn't spared the Pacific: repeated disasters, upended lives and heavy economic losses.

board.timeline_find: Each circle a disaster, placed at its year; the radius shows the scale.
board.timeline_take: Shocks aren't rare: they punctuate the whole period, and some hit very hard.
board.annual_find: Yearly total impact: the trend shows whether recent years weigh more.
board.annual_take: A few years carry most of the burden — the peaks dominate the total.
board.rank_find: Cumulative total per territory over the whole period (root scale keeps small ones visible).
board.rank_take: Impact is very unevenly spread: a handful of territories bears the most.
board.map_find: The geography of cumulative impact across the archipelago (log scale).
board.map_take: No one is spared, but the impact hotspots stand out clearly.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: The final act, and it looks ahead: the share of renewables in final energy consumption (%). Here, higher is better.

steps.v: 0% = all fossil; 100% = fully renewable energy. The bottom reference (0) is the starting point, not a normal.
The thick line is the regional average; the band shows where 8 in 10 territories sit (10th–90th percentile).
The slider (or ▶) moves the year on the curve AND the green 3D map.
The taller and greener a column, the higher the renewable share. The podium ranks the year's champions.

guide.takeaway: The momentum is real: driven by hydro, biomass and now solar, the renewable share is climbing — Kiribati, Fiji, Vanuatu, Samoa and the larger Melanesian islands leading the way.

act5.evo_sub: change in share between first and last year
act5.lead: The Pacific does not suffer in silence. Against rising seas and the human cost, a trajectory is taking hold: the share of renewable energy is climbing, territory by territory.
act5.gauge_sub: average renewable share of territories, in the current year
act5.change_sub: change in share between the first and last year
act5.dumb_sub: first → last observed year, per territory
act5.heat_sub: renewable share — the greener, the higher

story.trend: The regional mean climbs; the band shows the gap between pioneers and laggards.
story.gauge: In one figure, the region's renewable momentum — slide through the years to watch it rise.
story.map: On the map, green gains ground: some territories already draw most of their energy from renewables.
story.rank: In the chosen year, the champions' podium — often small territories with strong hydro/solar potential.
story.change: Not everyone moves at the same pace: a few territories leap ahead, others stall.
story.dumb: The distance travelled from one end of the period to the other: most segments stretch upward.
story.heat: Green intensifies from left to right: the transition reads at a glance.
story.evo: The strongest gains, in percentage points added.
story.table: Every value, sortable, to explore territory by territory.

outro.title: Momentum is building
outro.text: The transition is advancing, driven by a few pioneers. Now it must be fed — starting with what the land produces.

act5.thesis: Facing the threat, the Pacific moves: the share of renewable energy is climbing — unevenly, but the momentum is real.

board.trend_find: Renewable share of total final energy consumption (all uses: electricity, cooking, heating, transport). The band shows the regional spread (10–90%) around the mean.
board.trend_take: Momentum is real but uneven: the average rises, the gaps stay wide.
board.lines_find: Each line is a territory: renewable share of final energy consumption — who takes off, who stalls.
board.lines_take: A few pioneers pull the region forward; many stay fossil-dependent.
board.rank_find: Renewable share of final energy consumption, territory by territory, year after year: the bars reorder over time.
board.rank_take: The leading pack pulls clear — the transition doesn't move at the same pace everywhere.
board.map_find: Geography of the renewable share in final energy consumption for the selected year (green = greener).
board.map_take: Transition hotspots cluster; vast areas remain on fossil fuels.
board.evo_find: Who progressed most (and who fell back) on their renewable share of final energy consumption since the start.
board.evo_take: The biggest gains reveal the models the region could follow.
board.mix_band_find: Electricity generation (GWh) stacked, fossil against renewable, by year.
board.mix_band_take: At a glance, the renewable share eats into the fossil one over time.
board.mix_detail_find: Electricity output by source over the years (stacked columns); the total on top shows demand growth.
board.mix_detail_take: The detail shows which technologies drive the transition: solar and wind rising, thermal slowly receding.
board.mix_compo_find: Share of each source in every territory's generation (100% bars), for the selected year.
board.mix_compo_take: The contrast is striking: some territories run almost entirely on fossil fuels, others have already greened their mix.
board.mix_share_find: Renewable share of each territory's electricity generation, ranked, for the selected year.
board.mix_share_take: The ranking reveals the transition pioneers and those still locked into thermal power.
board.mix_race_find: Animated progression of the renewable share (%) in each territory's electricity generation, year by year.
board.mix_race_take: The transition plays out live: who pulls ahead, who catches up, who flips to clean power.
board.mix_tree_find: Share of each source in the selection's total electricity generation (area ∝ output), for the slider year.
board.mix_tree_take: Oil's weight is obvious; clean sources still cover only a fraction of the area.
board.mix_donut_find: Electricity mix composition of one territory (or region) for a given year: pick the area and year. The centre shows the renewable share.
board.mix_donut_take: Source by source, you see exactly what each island's power depends on — and how far there is to go.
board.mix_leader_find: Total electricity output of each source (bar length) for the slider year; the label shows the territory that produces the most and its share.
board.mix_leader_take: Oil dominates Pacific volume, but each source has its 'champion' — often a single territory carrying most of it.
board.mix_funnel_find: Total volume produced by each source across the WHOLE Pacific (funnel, largest to smallest), for the slider year.
board.mix_funnel_take: The funnel says it all: oil crushes the top, renewables share a narrow base.
board.mix_share_evo_find: Share of each source in the Pacific's total electricity generation (all territories combined), year by year (100% stacked bars). Hover = GWh produced.
board.mix_share_evo_take: A snapshot of the transition: renewables slowly chip away at fossil's share across the whole basin.

mix.band_title: Electricity: the greening mix — fossil vs renewable
mix.race_title: The transition in motion — renewable share
mix.tree_title: Where the power comes from — by source
mix.leader_title: By source: total volume and top consumer
mix.share_evo_title: Evolution of each source's share — Pacific

act6.title: The food-growing land under pressure
act6.lead: As the ocean rises and warms, the Pacific's plate wobbles too. Crop yields, territory by territory — what the region harvests, and how it shifts.

guide.title: Reading guide
guide.intro: We look at agricultural yield (kg harvested per hectare), summarised per territory as the MEDIAN across every crop tracked by the Pacific Data Hub — read with care (see the caveat below).

steps.v: Everything is in kg per hectare: the land's productivity, not total output.
Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia — to compare neighbouring territories.
The line shows each territory over time; the heatmap sums it all up on a colour grid.
The explorer drills down to each product (banana, taro, coconut…) via the icons.

guide.takeaway: Yields move — sometimes sharply — but rarely for a single reason. Climate is one factor among many.

act6.sub1_sub: Median yield per territory, across all crops — computed from real Pacific Data Hub data.
act6.heatmap_sub: each row = a territory · each column = a year · colour = yield
act6.change_sub: difference between the first and last available years
act6.explorer_lead: Pick a product to see its yield by territory and the most recent ranking.
act6.caveat_body: Agricultural yield does not depend on climate alone: farming practices, inputs, prices, planted area, pests and the estimation method weigh at least as much. Several series are also partly modelled (constant values) or have gaps. So we present this data as 'food systems under pressure', not as proof of a direct climate cause-and-effect.
act6.next: Coming next: livestock yield, then climate × yield cross-analysis.
act6.unavailable: Agricultural data momentarily unavailable from the Pacific Data Hub. Try again in a moment.
act6.trend_sub: One mini-chart per territory · each with its own scale (read the trend, not the level) · 1990–2024
act6.crop_rank_sub: Median yield per crop across the displayed territories (kg/ha) — moves with the timeline
act6.regional_sub: Median yield across all territories, year by year (kg/ha)
act6.diversity_sub: Number of distinct crops tracked per territory — higher means a more varied agricultural basket
act6.volatility_sub: Coefficient of variation of yields over time, by territory — higher means more exposed to shocks
act6.compare_sub: Median yield per territory between two chosen years — the bar links both values
act6.sub2_sub: Median yield per animal (kg/animal), territory by territory — from the same disaggregated dataset
act6.animal_rank_sub: Median yield per animal across the displayed territories (kg/animal)
act6.explorer_animal_lead: Pick an animal to see its yield by territory and the latest ranking.
act6.box_sub: spread across territories (Tukey box, log scale)
act6.cropmap_sub: yield by crop × territory, normalized per crop
act6.radar_sub: relative yield per crop (100 = best sub-region)

outro.title: Food security under strain
outro.text: Yields hinge on an ever more unstable climate. And nature itself — the living world — is on the front line.

act6.thesis: On fragile soils and a destabilising climate, feeding the Pacific becomes a challenge: yields waver, uneven from one island to the next.

board.small_find: Each mini-chart a territory: the path of its median yield, current point marked.
board.small_take: Yields don't converge: some rise, others stall or fall behind.
board.regional_find: The region's median yield, year after year.
board.regional_take: The overall trend hides wide disparities between territories.
board.crops_find: Crops ranked by median yield for the selected year.
board.crops_take: A few crops dominate; diversity remains a resilience challenge.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first year's yield linked to the last year's.
board.change_take: Gains and losses coexist — the climate doesn't pull the same way everywhere.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the yield at a glance.
board.heat_take: Good and bad years read as bands: production is climate-sensitive.
board.map_find: The geography of median yield for the chosen year (log scale).
board.map_take: Fertile lands and poor soils are very unevenly spread across the Pacific.
board.explorer_find: Pick a crop to explore its yield, territory by territory.
board.explorer_take: Behind the aggregate, each crop tells its own story of vulnerability.
board.stability_find: Coefficient of variation of yield over time, per territory (low = steady).
board.stability_take: The most volatile yields flag the production most exposed to climate shocks.
board.race_find: Pick a product: the race ranks territories by yield, year after year.
board.race_take: The leading pack reshuffles with the climate — no yield is guaranteed.
board.land_change_find: Change in the climate-altering land-cover index (CALCI) between 2015 (reference = 100) and the latest year, by territory.
board.land_change_take: A rise signals more altered land cover; several territories stay close to their 2015 level.
board.land_lines_find: Annual evolution of the CALCI index (2015 = 100) by territory, since 1992.
board.land_lines_take: A few territories saw land cover change sharply before converging; most evolve around the reference.
board.land_slope_find: CALCI index at the first and last observed year, by territory (2015 = 100).
board.land_slope_take: The slope sums up three decades of land-use change: steep for a few territories, near-flat for the rest.

act7.title: The living world retreats
act7.lead: As species fade, territories step up management measures. Two opposing curves: loss and response.
act7.redlist_sub: Red List Index (IUCN/BirdLife): 1 = pristine, 0 = all species extinct. It tracks aggregate extinction risk and declines across nearly every territory. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
act7.fish_sub: Number of fisheries management measures in place (multilateral and bilateral agreements), cumulative per territory — steadily rising. Source: Pacific Data Hub.
act7.unavailable: Living-world data temporarily unavailable from the Pacific Data Hub.
act7.rl_heatmap_sub: Heatmap of the index by territory and year ("trend" mode: each row on its own scale).
act7.rl_rank_sub: From least to most threatened. The lower the index, the higher the extinction risk.
act7.rl_compare_sub: Change in the index over three decades. ▼ = worsening (biodiversity loss).
act7.fish_heatmap_sub: Heatmap of measures in place by territory and year.
act7.fish_rank_sub: From most to least equipped. Larger Melanesia leads, smaller territories follow.
act7.fish_compare_sub: Growth in the number of measures since 2000. ▲ = stronger management.
act7.regional_rl_sub: Median Red List Index across visible territories, year by year. The slope falls: extinction risk worsens.
act7.regional_fish_sub: Median number of management measures in place across visible territories, year by year. The slope rises: governance strengthens.
act7.gauge_title: The health of life in one figure
act7.gauge_sub: median Red List Index of the selection (×100)
act7.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: fisheries management (X) vs Red List Index (Y)

story.rl_trend: The regional median Red List Index slips slowly downward: extinction risk is rising. The band shows the spread between territories.
story.fish_trend: Fisheries management measures pile up: an institutional response climbing, territory by territory.
story.gauge: The closer the index is to 100, the better life is preserved. Switch sub-region or territory to compare.
story.scatter: Crossing both indicators, we look for a link: does more fisheries management go hand in hand with a better Red List Index?

outro.title: Life on borrowed time
outro.text: Threatened species, strained fisheries: the balance is tightening. And above, the sky itself is changing.

act7.thesis: The Pacific's biodiversity is eroding and its fisheries are wavering: under climate pressure, the living world retreats, island after island.

board.trend_find: The regional median trajectory of the indicator, year after year.
board.trend_take: The overall trend slips slowly — pressure on nature isn't easing.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: No island escapes the trend, but the pace differs widely.
board.race_find: The territories' race on the indicator, year after year.
board.race_take: Positions barely move: inherited gaps persist over time.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first year's value linked to the last year's.
board.change_take: Setbacks and gains coexist — conservation pays where it's pursued.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the state of nature at a glance.
board.heat_take: Light/dark bands reveal territories under lasting pressure.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year (green = better preserved).
board.map_take: Pressure on the living world concentrates on certain archipelagos.

act8.title: The sky goes off-balance
act8.lead: The sea is rising — but the air and the rain are derailing too. Precipitation and temperature anomalies over Pacific lands, and the response: the station network that watches the sky.
act8.unavailable: Sky data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act8.credit: Sources: precipitation GPCP v2.3 (NOAA) · temperature NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 · network WMO/OSCAR (CC BY-SA 4.0). Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act8.rain_sub: Annual precipitation anomaly (mm) against the 1991–2020 normal. 0 = normal · negative = drier year · positive = wetter year.
act8.regional_rain_sub: Mean and spread (min–max band) of visible territories, year by year. The band shows how much islands diverge; 0 = the 1991–2020 normal.
act8.rain_hm_sub: Departure from the 1991–2020 normal, territory × year.
act8.rain_cmp_sub: Anomaly from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.temp_sub: Land surface temperature anomaly (°C, at 2 m) against the 1971–2000 normal. 0 = normal · negative = cooler · positive = warmer.
act8.regional_temp_sub: Mean and spread (min–max band) of visible territories, year by year. The slope rises: the land surface is warming; 0 = the 1971–2000 normal.
act8.temp_hm_sub: Departure from the 1971–2000 normal, territory × year.
act8.temp_cmp_sub: Anomaly from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.meteo_sub: Number of operational weather stations, cumulative year by year (WMO/OSCAR). The larger the network, the better the Pacific measures its own climate.
act8.regional_meteo_sub: Total operational stations across visible territories, year by year. The line rises: observing capacity strengthens.
act8.meteo_hm_sub: Number of stations by territory and year.
act8.meteo_rank_sub: Number of stations at the last available year; reference = median of visible territories.
act8.meteo_cmp_sub: Number of stations from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act8.rain_guide_intro: GPCP v2.3 data (NOAA), 2.5° grid, monthly totals aggregated to an annual total per country. The anomaly is the departure from the 1991–2020 reference mean (WMO standard).
act8.rain_guide_s1_v: The 0 line is the 1991–2020 normal. Above = wetter year, below = drier year.
act8.rain_guide_s2_v: The min–max band shows the spread across islands; each value also carries a standard error (uncertainty) computed from the year's monthly rainfall.
act8.rain_guide_s3_v: In millimetres: −20 means 20 mm below the normal over the year.
act8.rain_guide_takeaway: Example: Wallis & Futuna saw −20.5 in 1998 (a dry year) then +15 in 2017 (a wet year).
act8.temp_guide_intro: NOAAGlobalTemp v6.0.0 data, 5° grid, monthly land surface anomalies (at 2 m) aggregated to an annual mean per country. Reference: 1971–2000.
act8.temp_guide_s1_v: The 0 line is the 1971–2000 normal. Above = warmer than reference, below = cooler.
act8.temp_guide_s2_v: A standard error accompanies each value; early years (19th century) rest on statistical reconstruction, so they are more uncertain.
act8.temp_guide_s3_v: In degrees Celsius: +0.8 °C = land surface 0.8 °C above the normal.
act8.temp_guide_takeaway: Example: at Tokelau the anomaly moves from −0.94 °C in 1890 to +0.83 °C in 2024 — substantial warming.
act8.meteo_guide_intro: WMO data, OSCAR database. Only operational stations are counted; the indicator adds 1 per active year of each station, between opening and closure (or today).
act8.meteo_guide_s1_v: We rebuild each territory's total by summing station types (PDH also provides the breakdown by type).
act8.meteo_guide_s2_v: Conservative filtering (excluding 'Silent' or 'Unknown' stations) may underestimate the network but keeps the data robust.
act8.meteo_guide_s3_v: The higher the count, the stronger the capacity to observe the climate — this is the response to the disruptions.
act8.meteo_guide_takeaway: Example: in Samoa, 1 station in 1889, 4 stations in 2025.
act8.box_sub: range of anomalies, year by year
act8.share_warm_sub: share of territories with a positive anomaly (warmer than normal)
act8.share_wet_sub: share of territories with a positive anomaly (wetter than normal)
act8.gauge_sub: share with a positive temperature anomaly
act8.scatter_title: Do warming and rainfall move together?
act8.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: temperature anomaly (X) × precipitation anomaly (Y)

story.rain_trend: The regional median drifts from normal (line 0); the band shows the spread between dry and wet territories.
story.temp_trend: The temperature anomaly drifts above normal: warming reads directly against the 0 line.
story.box: Each box summarises a year: median, interquartile range and outliers across territories.
story.share_wet: The share of territories wetter than normal, year by year — is exposure becoming widespread?
story.share_warm: The share of territories warmer than normal climbs: warming becomes nearly universal.
story.gauge: In the latest year, the proportion of territories above the thermal normal. Switch sub-region to compare.
story.scatter: Crossing both anomalies, we look for a pattern: does warming go with more drought or more rain?

outro.title: A drifting sky
outro.text: Rain and heat pull lastingly away from normal. This disruption has a cost — economic, this time.

act8.thesis: Above the Pacific, the sky is drifting: disrupted rainfall and temperatures pulling away from normal, year after year.

board.trend_find: The regional anomaly (mean + min–max spread), year after year, against the baseline.
board.trend_take: The gap from normal widens: the climate is drifting beyond natural variability.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: The drift is general, but some islands deviate far more than others.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the deviation from normal (blue below, red above).
board.heat_take: Recent warm bands betray a lasting shift in the regime.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first year's value linked to the last year's.
board.change_take: Almost everywhere the needle pushes the same way — a coherent signal.
board.race_find: The territories' race on the indicator, year after year.
board.race_take: Observation density grows — but stays uneven across archipelagos.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year.
board.map_take: Disruption isn't uniform: the map reveals the hotspots.

act9.title: The exposed economy
act9.lead: Behind the climate, whole economies. The tourism that keeps the islands alive, the electricity they produce, and green taxation — a lever still barely used.
act9.unavailable: Economic data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act9.credit: Sources: tourism UN Tourism (CC-BY) · electricity IMF/IRENA · taxation IMF/OECD. Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act9.tour_sub: Visitor arrivals (counts), source UN Tourism. An economic backbone of the islands — and among the first hit by cyclones, damaged reefs and global shocks.
act9.regional_tour_sub: Sum of arrivals across visible territories, year by year. The 2020–2021 crater reveals how fragile a heavily dependent model is.
act9.tour_rank_sub: Arrivals at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act9.tour_cmp_sub: Arrivals from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act9.power_sub: Electricity generation (GWh), source IMF/IRENA. Includes grid, off-grid and self-consumption by energy industries.
act9.regional_power_sub: Sum of generation across visible territories, year by year. Demand climbs: growing pressure on the energy mix.
act9.power_hm_sub: GWh by territory and year (row-normalised, log scale).
act9.power_rank_sub: Generation at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act9.power_cmp_sub: Generation from the first to the last available year, by territory.
act9.tax_sub: Environmental taxes as % of GDP (Energy, Transport, Pollution, Resources), source IMF/OECD. Very limited coverage: only a few countries report — that scarcity is the point.
act9.regional_tax_sub: Median of reporting territories, year by year. The line stays low: the fiscal climate lever is barely used.
act9.tax_sm_title: Each reporting territory, year by year
act9.tax_hm_sub: Total environmental taxes (% GDP) by territory and year.
act9.tax_rank_sub: Peak environmental taxes (% of GDP) reached over the whole period; reference = median of peaks. Territories with no documented tax are omitted.
act9.tax_cmp_sub: Total environmental taxes (% GDP) from the first to the last available year.
act9.tour_guide_intro: UN Tourism data (arrival statistics). A tourist = a visitor staying at least one night; the total also includes same-day excursionists.
act9.tour_guide_s1_v: Absolute counts: 200,000 = 200,000 arrivals in the year.
act9.tour_guide_s2_v: The sharp 2020–2021 drop (COVID) shows how these economies hang by a single thread.
act9.tour_guide_s3_v: Bleached reefs, eroded beaches, more frequent cyclones: the climate directly threatens tourism appeal.
act9.tour_guide_takeaway: Example: French Polynesia went from 201,000 arrivals (1997) to 261,400 (2022).
act9.power_guide_intro: IMF (climate dashboard) data sourced from IRENA. The total sums all technologies and grid-connection types per country-year.
act9.power_guide_s1_v: In gigawatt-hours (GWh): the energy produced over the year.
act9.power_guide_s2_v: Includes grid-connected, off-grid and electricity self-consumed by energy industries.
act9.power_guide_s3_v: Rising generation = rising demand, hence a transition challenge toward renewables (see Act 5).
act9.power_guide_takeaway: Example: Fiji rose from about 673 GWh (2000) to 1,285 GWh (2023).
act9.tax_guide_intro: IMF (climate dashboard) data sourced from the OECD. Only percentage-of-GDP observations are kept, broken down by tax type (Energy, Transport, Pollution, Resources).
act9.tax_guide_s1_v: As % of GDP: 0.89 means 0.89% of GDP comes from environmental taxes.
act9.tax_guide_s2_v: Extremely limited coverage (~44% missing; only a handful of countries). Few territories pull this lever.
act9.tax_guide_s3_v: The total is reliable only when all categories are documented for a country-year; we obtain it by summing the types.
act9.tax_guide_takeaway: Example: in Fiji, the weight of these taxes goes from 0.89% of GDP (2011) to 0.4% (2020).
act9.scatter_title: Do tourism and energy move together?
act9.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: arrivals (X) × electricity generation (Y)
act9.gauge_title: Is the fiscal lever being used?

story.tour_tree: Area shows the share: at a glance, which territories concentrate arrivals.
story.tour_pareto: The cumulative line climbs fast: a handful of destinations makes up most of regional tourism.
story.scatter: Crossing tourism and electricity reveals economic size: are the big destinations also the most energy-hungry?
story.gauge: Environmental taxation, as a share of GDP, stays modest: a lever barely pulled. Switch sub-region to compare.

outro.title: An exposed economy
outro.text: Tourism and energy concentrate wealth as much as vulnerability. At the end of the chain: people's health.

act9.thesis: The Pacific economy — tourism, energy, taxation — rests on a narrow, dependent base, on the front line of climate shocks.

board.trend_find: The regional trajectory of the indicator, year after year.
board.trend_take: The Pacific economy rests on a narrow base, highly exposed to shocks.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: A few territories concentrate most; the rest stay marginal.
board.race_find: The territories' race on the indicator, year after year.
board.race_take: The ranking barely moves: economic positions are lastingly entrenched.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first year's value linked to the last year's.
board.change_take: Growth and decline coexist — economic resilience isn't shared.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the level at a glance.
board.heat_take: Ruptures (shocks, crises) read as dark bands across the grid.
board.radar_find: The average profile of each sub-region, decade by decade.
board.radar_take: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia don't follow the same economic model.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year.
board.map_take: Economic wealth concentrates on a few island hubs.

act10.title: The body and the water
act10.lead: As close to people as it gets: the water they drink and the disease that stalks them. The last step before the synthesis — where climate meets daily health.
act10.unavailable: Health data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act10.credit: Sources: water WHO/UNICEF (JMP) · tuberculosis WHO (Global TB Report 2024). Data: Pacific Data Hub (SPC).
act10.water_sub: Share of the population using safely managed drinking water services (%), source WHO/UNICEF. A fragile resource: saltwater intrusion into atoll aquifers, droughts, networks destroyed by cyclones.
act10.regional_water_sub: Median of visible territories, year by year. Here, higher is better: the goal is 100%.
act10.water_hm_sub: % of population by territory and year (row-normalised). Darker means higher access.
act10.water_rank_sub: % at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act10.water_cmp_sub: Access to safe water from the first to the last available year. Rising = progress.
act10.tb_sub: Tuberculosis incidence per 100,000 people, source WHO. A marker of vulnerability (poverty, crowding, nutrition): climate acts mostly indirectly, through displacement and shocks.
act10.regional_tb_sub: Median of visible territories, year by year. Here, lower is better: a decline is progress.
act10.tb_hm_sub: Cases / 100,000 by territory and year (row-normalised). Darker means a heavier burden.
act10.tb_rank_sub: Cases / 100,000 at the last known value; reference = median of visible territories.
act10.tb_cmp_sub: Incidence from the first to the last available year. Falling = improvement.
act10.water_guide_title: How to read — safe drinking water
act10.water_guide_intro: WHO/UNICEF data (Joint Monitoring Programme, JMP). A 'safely managed' service = water accessible at home, available when needed and free from contamination.
act10.water_guide_s1_v: As % of population: 95 means 95% of people have safe water.
act10.water_guide_s2_v: Higher is better. A rising line = progress; a falling one (as in Solomon Islands) = a retreat to watch.
act10.water_guide_s3_v: On atolls, rising seas salinise aquifers; droughts and cyclones cut access. Safe water is on the front line.
act10.water_guide_takeaway: Example: Papua New Guinea went from about 32% (2000) to 50% (2022), while Solomon Islands fell from 79% to 67%.
act10.tb_guide_intro: WHO data (Global Tuberculosis Report 2024). Incidence measures estimated new cases each year, relative to population.
act10.tb_guide_s1_v: As cases per 100,000 people: 100 = 100 new cases a year per 100,000 people.
act10.tb_guide_s2_v: Lower is better. A decline is progress. On the maps, a darker cell = a heavier burden.
act10.tb_guide_s3_v: For very small populations (Niue, Tokelau…), a single case sends the rate soaring: sharp swings there must be taken with caution. The climate link is indirect.
act10.tb_guide_takeaway: Example: Vanuatu cut its incidence from 110 (2000) to 41 (2023); conversely, it climbs in Kiribati (~355 → 533).
act10.box_sub: median, interquartile range and outliers, by year
act10.scatter_title: Does water shield from the burden?
act10.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory: drinking-water access (X) × tuberculosis (Y)

story.water_band: The line tracks the regional median; the p10–p90 band shows the gap between best- and least-served territories.
story.tb_band: The regional median incidence; the p10–p90 band reveals the wide gaps in burden between territories.
story.box: Each box summarises a year: median, interquartile range and outliers across territories.
story.gauge: The median drinking-water access of the selection. The fuller the arc, the better. Switch sub-region to compare.
story.scatter: Crossing water access and tuberculosis, we look for the expected link: does better sanitation go with a lower burden?

outro.title: Health under strain
outro.text: Water, disease: health fragilities stack onto the other pressures. Time to connect it all.

act10.thesis: Pacific health remains fragile: still-incomplete access to water and persistent diseases, worsened by climate disruption.

board.trend_find: The regional median trajectory of the indicator, year after year.
board.trend_take: Health gains are real but slow — and fragile in the face of shocks.
board.multiples_find: Each mini-chart a territory: its own trajectory, current point marked.
board.multiples_take: Health gaps between islands stay deep and persistent.
board.race_find: The territories' race on the indicator, year after year.
board.race_take: The ranking barely moves: health inequalities are lastingly entrenched.
board.change_find: For each territory, the first year's value linked to the last year's.
board.change_take: A few territories progress markedly; others stall or fall back.
board.heat_find: Territories × years: intensity shows the level at a glance.
board.heat_take: The bands reveal territories in lasting difficulty.
board.radar_find: The average profile of each sub-region, decade by decade.
board.radar_take: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia don't advance at the same health pace.
board.map_find: The geography of the indicator for the last year.
board.map_take: Access to care and water draws a deeply unequal map of the Pacific.

act11.title: Guilty of almost nothing, exposed to almost everything
act11.lead: Ten acts, one thread. Here we cross everything we've seen: what these islands emit, and what they bear. Responsibility on one side, vulnerability on the other — and the gap that lays bare the Pacific's climate injustice.
act11.unavailable: Synthesis data momentarily unavailable. Please try again shortly.
act11.credit: Sources: Pacific Data Hub (SPC) — GHG/capita, sea level & SST (IMF, NOAA), precipitation (GPCP), drinking water (WHO/UNICEF), tuberculosis (WHO), Red List (IUCN).
act11.credit_partial: Some indicators were unavailable at load time: the index is computed on those that responded.
act11.scatter_sub: each dot = a territory · X: GHG per capita · Y: vulnerability index
act11.rank_sub: composite index, most to least exposed
act11.profile_sub: Index broken down by dimension (0–100, 100 = the Pacific's worst on that dimension). Pick 'All' to return to the ranking.
act11.guide_title: How to read — the vulnerability index
act11.guide_intro: We bring together six signals seen in earlier acts — sea level, sea-temperature anomaly, rainfall deviation, drinking-water access, tuberculosis, Red List Index — for each territory, at their last known value.
act11.guide_s1_v: Each indicator is rescaled to 0–100 (the Pacific's worst = 100), with the right polarity: water and biodiversity are inverted (low = worse), rainfall counted as absolute deviation. The index is the equal-weight average of available dimensions.
act11.guide_s2_v: Right of the scatter = emits more per head; up = more exposed. The top-left quadrant gathers those who weigh little on the climate yet suffer it most — the heart of the injustice.
act11.guide_s3_v: This is not an official index: equal weights by choice, normalisation relative to the Pacific (not the world), volatile small populations, and a territory missing a dimension is averaged over the others. Read it as a narrative, not a verdict.
act11.guide_takeaway: The overall pattern fits in one line: the smallest emitters are rarely the least exposed — often the opposite.
act11.stat_inj_sub: territories below the world average AND above the vulnerability median
act11.cross_sub: one side emits, the other bears it — let's confront responsibility and vulnerability
act11.bubble_sub: X: GHG/capita · Y: vulnerability · bubble size: exposure to sea-level rise
act11.slope_sub: each line links responsibility (left) to vulnerability (right), 0 to 100
act11.donut_sub: each sub-region's share of the Pacific's cumulative vulnerability
act11.treemap_sub: each tile's area is proportional to the vulnerability index
act11.pareto_sub: GHG per capita, highest to lowest, with cumulative share
act11.fingerprint_sub: each territory has its own vulnerability signature
act11.matrix_sub: territories × six dimensions · green = low, red = high (0–100)
act11.radar_sub: average vulnerability per dimension — Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia
act11.polar_sub: the six dimensions fanned out as sectors (0–100)
act11.radial_sub: vulnerability index of the six leading territories
act11.gauge_sub: median composite index of the current selection

story.scatter: Read left to right: the further left, the fewer emissions. The higher up, the more vulnerable. The vertical line marks the world average, the horizontal one the median vulnerability. The top-left corner — few emissions, high exposure — is the very face of climate injustice.
story.bubble: Same plane, but bubble size now adds exposure to sea-level rise. The big bubbles clustered on the left say it plainly: those hit hardest by the sea are not those who loaded the atmosphere.
story.slope: Each line starts at a territory's responsibility (left) and reaches its vulnerability (right). An upward slope (coral) = few emissions but high exposure. The thicket of rising slopes sums up the whole act.
story.donut: Cumulative vulnerability isn't shared evenly: one sub-region holds a markedly larger slice. The ring shows at a glance where the risk weighs most.
story.treemap: The bigger the tile, the more vulnerable the territory. The hierarchy jumps out without reading a single figure.
story.pareto: Bars rank the emitters, the line accumulates their share. Its fast rise shows responsibility concentrates on a handful of territories.
story.matrix: One row per territory, one column per dimension. Red bands reveal the pressure points: some pile up fragilities, others have just one or two critical ones.
story.radar: Each polygon's shape tells where a sub-region hurts most: the sea here, water or health there. Three geographies of risk, overlaid.
story.polar: The six dimensions open out like a fan. The longer a sector, the more that threat weighs in the chosen profile — a single territory, or the selection's average.
story.radial: The rings compare the leading territories: their composite index, side by side, to see the gap among the most exposed.
story.gauge: The median vulnerability level of the selection. Switch sub-region or territory and watch the needle move.
story.rank: The full vulnerability ranking. In territory mode it becomes the breakdown of that territory's six dimensions, with 50 = the average.
story.gap: Here is the debt, plainly: for each territory, subtract responsibility (its emissions) from vulnerability (what it bears). The long coral bars to the right are the territories paying the heaviest price for a disruption they didn't cause. The cyan bars on the left are the reverse — rare in the Pacific.
story.mirror: On the left what a territory emits, on the right what it bears, back to back. When the right wing (coral) dwarfs the left (cyan), the injustice is obvious: much exposure for very little responsibility.
story.sloperank: The same reversal, by rank: left, the emitters' ranking (1 = most responsible); right, the most vulnerable. The lines that plunge — high on the right, low on the left — are the most unjustly exposed territories.
story.voyage_text: Before the verdict, let's retrace the path — each act, a piece of the Pacific's climate puzzle.
story.resp_title: Tiny emitters, on the front line
story.resp_text: The Pacific is among the world's lowest per-capita CO₂ emitters. Yet its territories bear the brunt of rising seas, cyclones and erosion. The responsibility lies elsewhere; the bill is paid here.
story.ocean_text: Decade after decade, sea level rises across the three sub-regions. For low-lying atolls, every millimetre counts.
story.atlas_text: Crossing six climate stresses, a map emerges: some islands stack up every pressure at once.
story.radar_text: Melanesia, Polynesia, Micronesia don't carry the same vulnerabilities — but none is spared.
story.matrix_text: Territories in rows, stresses in columns. Red accumulates: it's not one isolated risk, it's their sum.
story.paradox_title: Pollute the least, suffer the most
story.paradox_text: Responsibility on X, vulnerability on Y. The Pacific sits where the injustice is starkest.
story.focus_hint: Click a territory to highlight it.
story.recap_a2: Ocean — sea level and heat are rising.
story.recap_a3: Territory — populations concentrated on the coasts.
story.recap_a7: Living world — biodiversity and fisheries in retreat.
story.recap_a9: Economy — tourism and energy, exposed wealth.
story.recap_a10: Health — water and disease, persistent fragilities.
story.recap_a11: Synthesis — it all converges: the climate debt.

outro.title: A debt they never took on
outro.text: Eleven acts, dozens of datasets, one conclusion: those who pay for the climate aren't those who disrupted it. The rest is up to us.

act11.method: Method: latest value per indicator, normalised 0–100 (min-max), then averaged with equal weights. Indicative index, not official.
act11.gap_sub: vulnerability − responsibility, per territory · right = bears more than it owes
act11.mirror_title: Emits on one side, bears it on the other
act11.thesis: Here is the verdict of the whole journey: the Pacific emitted almost nothing, yet stacks up vulnerabilities. A climate debt it never took on.

board.paradox_find: Each dot a territory: its responsibility (GHG/capita, X) against its composite vulnerability (Y).
board.paradox_take: The injustice is stark: the Pacific emits very little, yet ranks among the most vulnerable.
board.vuln_find: Composite index (0–100): normalized average of the six climate stresses, per territory.
board.vuln_take: The most exposed stack sea-level rise, heat, water and health strain.
board.matrix_find: Each row a territory, each column a stress: green = spared, red = under pressure.
board.matrix_take: No single vulnerability: it's the accumulation of stresses that makes the danger.
board.radar_find: The average profile of each sub-region across the six vulnerability dimensions.
board.radar_take: Melanesia, Polynesia and Micronesia carry different but very real fragilities.
board.resp_find: GHG emissions per capita, ranked: each one's share of responsibility.
board.resp_take: Even the Pacific's biggest emitters stay far below the world average.

studio.sec_atlas_hint: Size = vulnerability, color = chosen layer. Click a territory: it highlights everywhere.
studio.sec_score_hint: Regional median of the composite index.
studio.sec_score_hint_sel: Composite index of the selected territory.
studio.sec_radar_hint: Average profile of the three sub-regions.
studio.sec_radar_hint_sel: Selected territory compared to its sub-region.
studio.sec_paradox_hint: Responsibility (X) against vulnerability (Y). Shaded quadrant: low emissions, high exposure. Click a dot.
studio.sec_matrix_hint: Territories × climate stresses. Green = spared, red = under pressure. Click a row.
studio.sec_tree_hint: Area = emissions/capita, color = vulnerability. Click a tile.
studio.sec_rank_hint: The composite index, most to least exposed.
studio.sec_trend_hint: Sea level, median per sub-region, year after year.
studio.insight_empty: Select a territory to reveal its profile.
studio.insight_ov2: more CO₂/capita than the Pacific, yet
studio.insight_ov3: territories emit little but rank among the most vulnerable.

about.title: Datamoana, and the hand behind the data
about.lead: An independent data-visualisation project built for the Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026: making the Pacific's climate data legible, at human scale.

author.name: Stoyann Velten
author.role: Developer & IT support — Nouméa, New Caledonia
author.body: I live and work in Nouméa, New Caledonia, as a developer and IT support in one of the territory's large hardware groups. I built Datamoana on my own time: the Pacific is home, and these numbers are about our daily life — the sea eating at the coast, cyclones, water, the reefs. My goal: make them clear and tangible, without cheating the scales, so anyone can grasp at a glance what our islands are going through.
author.note: Solo project · React, ECharts & Mapbox GL · Pacific Data Hub (SPC) data

data.title: Open, verifiable sources
data.lead: All of Datamoana's data comes from open, public sources, aggregated by the Pacific Community's (SPC) Pacific Data Hub .Stat. It matches the challenge's official list: each act shows its source, unit and method.
data.platform_desc: Unified access point (SDMX API) to the Pacific's regional statistics.
data.note: Technical access via the SDMX API: stats-sdmx-disseminate.pacificdata.org
data.integrity: Data transparency — All visualisations rely on real data fetched live from the Pacific Data Hub and official sources. When data is unavailable, the app shows “data unavailable” rather than estimated values: nothing is fabricated or hard-coded. The only documented exception — the coastline (Digital Earth Pacific — Landsat Coastlines, CC BY-NC 4.0), a large geospatial dataset provided as a precomputed extract; its per-territory figures (retreat, growth, spread) are an approximate aggregation, obtained by attaching each coastline segment to the nearest territory. Territory coordinates are used only to place points on the map.

challenge.title: Pacific Dataviz Challenge 2026
challenge.body: Datamoana is my entry to the 2026 Pacific Dataviz Challenge, whose theme is climate change in the Pacific. Eleven acts, 23 official datasets crossed, one story.

story.title: The power of a well-told dataset
story.p1: I'm passionate about what data reveals once you stage it: it turns numbers into decisions — sometimes into anticipation.
story.walmart: The example that opened my eyes: before a hurricane, Walmart mined years of sales and found strawberry Pop-Tarts selling about 7× the usual rate (beer led the list). Stores pre-stocked in the storm's path, shelves cleared.
story.p3: Picture that same anticipation for our cyclone watches in the Pacific: knowing, before the storm, what each island will need. That's the power of data — and the spirit of Datamoana.

tpl.biggest_change: For {metric}, {area} went from {v0} to {v1} between {year0} and {year1}.
tpl.region_top: For {metric}, {area} is the highest sub-region ({value} on average).
